_meta.description: Typical UMF limit ranges for functional glossy glazes, as heuristics for flagging a recipe that's likely to craze, be under-durable, or misbehave. These are GUIDANCE from common ceramics references (e.g. Hesselberth & Roy 'Mastering Cone 6 Glazes' for the cone-6 set), not hard rules — plenty of good glazes sit outside them. Values are UMF (fluxes = 1.0). ratios are molar.
_meta.source: Compiled from common glaze-chemistry references. Approximate.

notes.SiO2_Al2O3: Lower ratio (~5-7) tends matte/silky; higher (~8-12) glossier/glassier. Very high with low alumina risks running and low durability.
notes.Al2O3: Too low → runny, soft, less durable. High alumina (>~0.5 with lower silica) → alumina matte.
notes.B2O3: A melter that also lowers thermal expansion. Useful to fix crazing without adding high-expansion alkalis.
notes.crazing: Glaze contracts more than the clay body on cooling → cracks. Lower expansion: cut KNaO (Na2O worst), add SiO2, add B2O3, swap some flux to MgO or Li2O.
notes.shivering: Glaze contracts less than body → flakes off edges. Raise expansion: more KNaO, less SiO2/B2O3.
notes.durability: Very high alkali (KNaO) with low SiO2/Al2O3 leaches — avoid for food surfaces. Keep silica and alumina toward the upper end for functional ware.
